summary: Ronacher responds to Glyph’s opt‑out stance by narrowing the debate to code and utilitarian text, where he sees AI as a pragmatic productivity tool rather than a creative substitute. He argues that differences in experience come from how people learn to use the tools, and he reports overwhelmingly positive results with agentic workflows. He disputes the idea that adoption is purely top‑down, pointing to grassroots uptake even before companies pay for it. On climate and copyright, he views increased energy demand as manageable within broader electrification trends and sees broader scraping as aligned with open sharing. He believes LLMs can improve learning and that cheating signals flaws in assessment, not learning itself. His conclusion is to accept AI’s trajectory, work alongside it, and stop feeling compelled to justify the choice.
key_points: Utility matters: code and functional text are different from art, so AI’s use there is easier to justify.
Skillful use drives quality; his success comes from learning how to guide tools effectively.
Adoption is often grassroots, not only executive‑mandated, with employees paying out of pocket.
Energy and copyright concerns are real but not disqualifying; increased demand may accelerate renewables and broader scraping is a net positive.
LLMs can enhance learning; cheating reflects assessment problems more than a failure of learning.
money_quotes: For me, it's not just “neutral-to-positive” — it's been astonishingly positive.
This doesn't match what I've seen. In my experience, people are adopting AI on their own, often before their companies are even aware.
It's quite freeing really and it does have a different feeling to me than a regular code review.
So is programming still enjoyable if I don't hit the keys? For me, yes.
I've always felt that copyright terms are too long, scraping is beneficial, and sharing knowledge is a net positive for humanity.
AI might just make these pre-existing problems more visible.
The path for me is pretty clear: it's going towards me working together with the machine.
key_insight: If you treat AI as a utilitarian collaborator for code and learning, the practical gains outweigh the moral anxieties, and the healthiest response is to accept its trajectory and work with it.
